main_summary.summary: Esther M. A. Terry, interviewed July 6, 2011, on the campus of Bennett College by Joe Mosnier for the Civil Rights History Project, recounts her path from a sharecropping family in Wise, Warren County, North Carolina, to the Greensboro sit-ins of 1960 and on to the founding of W. E. B. Du Bois African American Studies at UMass Amherst. The youngest of twelve, Terry describes her mother Mary Alexander's commitment to schooling and her father's purchase of the family farm in 1945. She entered Bennett College on a scholarship after the 1957 extension of Social Security to farmers gave her parents a check. At Bennett she studied English under Hobart Jarrett, grammar under Dr. Crawford, theater under Fred Eady, and lived in the Honors Dorm under the leadership of Dr. Willa B. Player. Terry participated in the Greensboro sit-ins at the F.W. Woolworth lunch counter beginning February 1, 1960, alongside Bennett College student activists Roslyn Smith, Gwen Mackel, Shirley Dismute, Dolores Finger, Gloria Brown, and Linda Brown. After graduating in spring 1961 she earned a master's at UNC-Chapel Hill and a doctorate at UMass Amherst, where she helped build the African American Studies program with Michael Thelwell, Bernard W. Bell, Eugene Terry, and Sidney Kaplan. She returned to Bennett as Provost in 2009.
key_themes: Greensboro sit-ins and Bennett College student activists
Black women's education at a historically Black private college
Dr. Willa B. Player's institutional leadership and protection of student protest
Founding of African American Studies at UMass Amherst
Family farm, Social Security extension to farmers, and access to higher education
main_summary.historical_significance:
Terry's testimony documents the planning role of Bennett College women in the Greensboro sit-ins, a counter-narrative to the standard account that centers only the four North Carolina A&T State University men who sat down on February 1, 1960. She describes a deliberated plan that originated among Bennett sociology students, was reviewed by Dr. Hobart Jarrett and Dr. Willa B. Player, and was timed by Player to follow Christmas vacation so that participants would not scatter mid-campaign. The interview also records Player's drive to the Greensboro jail to claim arrested Bennett students and the cross-burning at the president's house.

Terry's account of UMass Amherst documents the founding pipeline of one of the first PhD-granting African American Studies departments in the country: the Sterling A. Brown-to-Sidney Kaplan referral chain out of Howard University, the prior 1959 dedication of The Massachusetts Review to the A&T Four, and the multiracial faculty-student coalition that secured department status without the protest violence that marked similar efforts at peer institutions.

chapters.part: A Family of Twelve in Warren County
chapters.title: The Youngest of Twelve
chapters.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier at Bennett College in Greensboro on July 6, 2011, Provost Esther Terry opens by describing her childhood as the youngest of twelve on a farm, surrounded by older siblings, and begins the story of her mother, who entered normal school to become a teacher but was called home when her own mother died.

chapters.part: A Family of Twelve in Warren County
chapters.title: Two Widowed by the Flu
chapters.summary: Terry recounts how her mother's first husband died in the flu epidemic of 1917, leaving her with three surviving children in Richmond, Virginia, while her father lost his first wife and three children to the same epidemic; widowed, her mother returned to Warren County, met her father, and he raised all the children as his own, with nine more born to the union.

chapters.part: A Family of Twelve in Warren County
chapters.title: The Land in Wise, North Carolina
chapters.summary: She describes how her father, a sharecropper, worked with the owner to buy the land around 1945, land the family has kept ever since; her father died in 1970 and her mother in 1985, and now the last of her siblings, she holds the farm in Wise, North Carolina for the nieces and nephews who are her many children.

chapters.part: A Family of Twelve in Warren County
chapters.title: A Sermon Rather Than a Preaching
chapters.summary: Terry explains her churchgoing mother valued the teachings of the church over its preachings, favoring Sunday school and the saying that she would rather see a sermon than hear one, and recalls being asked each week what she had learned and her mother's refrain, how would you feel if that happened to you.

chapters.part: A Family of Twelve in Warren County
chapters.title: Run, Run to the Field
chapters.summary: She recalls loving school and going because her mother thought it important, though farm work kept the children out when the crops needed saving; her mother picked cotton to make up for their absence, and the children were sent straight from school to change clothes and run to the field while their mother went to make supper.

chapters.part: A Family of Twelve in Warren County
chapters.title: The Church and the School as Centers
chapters.summary: Terry describes how the church and the school served as centers of the spread-out Black farming community around Wise, with parents deeply involved in Easter and evening programs, and notes that whether the family could attend often depended on whether a brother was home to drive, since her father never learned to drive.

chapters.part: A Family of Twelve in Warren County
chapters.title: The Luckiest, Not the Smartest
chapters.summary: As the first in her family to attend a four-year college, Terry reflects that she was not the smartest but the luckiest of her siblings, since there was no money and few scholarships; her brothers and sisters left for factory work, two at Ford Motors, one at a Newport News shipyard, and one in the New York school system, taking pride in their jobs and buying their homes.

chapters.part: A Family of Twelve in Warren County
chapters.title: The Black Community Was the Bible
chapters.summary: Terry explains that farmers gained Social Security in 1957, retroactive to age sixty-five, and how she helped neighbors born at home with midwives prove their ages through family Bibles where births and deaths were recorded, and through catastrophic events like floods and tornadoes that served as landmarks for dating their lives.

chapters.part: A Family of Twelve in Warren County
chapters.title: This One Will Go to College
chapters.topic: A Mother's Hope and a We
chapters.summary: Terry recalls that when her father got a Social Security check her mother declared this one would go to college, revealing she had wanted every child to go to school; she describes evening spelling contests and lessons as a family, and how at seventeen her ambitions were shaped less by independent plans than by a collective we centered on owning the land.

chapters.title: The Childhood Story Retold
chapters.summary: After a pause the recording restarts and Terry re-tells the childhood material covered earlier, again describing being the youngest of twelve, her parents widowed by the 1917 flu, the family's purchase of the land around 1945, her churchgoing mother's teachings, picking cotton, her siblings' factory jobs, Social Security in 1957 and proving neighbors' ages by the family Bible, and her mother's resolve that she would go to college.

chapters.title: Born Up by the Community
chapters.summary: Asked about Theaoseus Tobias Clayton of Warren County, whom she later knew, Terry turns to her arrival at Bennett College fifty-seven years earlier, describing how the whole community prayed for her and gave her a few pennies, how she had earned a small scholarship on the SAT, and how her parents drove her to a campus she had never seen.

chapters.title: The Mothers Who Exchanged Children
chapters.summary: Terry recalls the lonely feeling when parents said goodbye, and how, as they settled her in, her mother met the mother of Roslyn Smith from West Virginia; because the Smiths could not afford trips home, the two mothers bonded and exchanged children, with Terry's family hosting Roslyn for holidays across all four years, beginning a friendship that endures to this day.

chapters.title: Miss Alexander, What Am I Holding
chapters.summary: Studying English and theater in the late 1950s, Terry honors her professors, the brilliant and exacting Hobart Jarrett, who quizzed her by holding up a book and demanding its title and convinced his arrogant students they could write and debate anything, and the less polished grammar terror Dr. Crawford, who drilled them in parsing and diagramming sentences.

chapters.title: We Sent Him the Flowers
chapters.summary: Terry tells how the theater director Fred Eady came from Howard and how the students, deciding he should marry, secretly bought flowers for a woman on campus and signed his name until the two did marry; she recalls living in Honors Dorm and being taught daily that Bennett girls were as good as anyone and bound to give back to their communities.

chapters.part: Safety Inside the Gate, Signs Outside
chapters.title: Happiness Was Being Safe
chapters.summary: Terry reflects that her childhood happiness was bound up with feeling safe, even as the Southern terrain was dotted with white-only signs, and describes Bennett as a cocoon where students were taught they were special; she frames the contradiction of F.W. Woolworth, where a Bennett lady could buy anything but could not sit down at the lunch counter for a cherry coke.

chapters.part: Safety Inside the Gate, Signs Outside
chapters.title: Aware of the Storm
chapters.summary: Asked how much attention she gave to wider events like Little Rock, early Greensboro school desegregation, and the lawsuits of figures like Dr. Simkins, Terry says Bennett's purpose was always to prepare students to contribute to the world, that her classmates came with prior experience of racial conflict, and that they felt safe inside even as a storm raged around them.

chapters.part: Safety Inside the Gate, Signs Outside
chapters.title: She Erased the T and the Y
chapters.summary: Terry recounts her mother's rule against speaking of all white people as they, demonstrated by writing THEY on a board and erasing letters until only he and she remained, insisting wrongs be named to individuals; she repeats her mother's lesson to her son that white people were as good as they knew how to be.

chapters.title: What If We Sat Down
chapters.summary: Terry describes how by the fall of 1959 her sociology-studying classmates, among them Roslyn Smith, Gwen Mackel, Shirley Dismute, and Gloria Brown, were intensely engaged and brought their discussions back to the dorms, asking how barriers could be torn down, what would happen if they boycotted or picketed, and finally what would happen if they sat down at the lunch counter, talking through nonviolence all the while.

chapters.title: It Was Not Spontaneous
chapters.summary: Terry argues the Bennett women are not sufficiently credited, recounting a fiftieth-anniversary retrospective where Gwen Mackel, Shirley Dismute, Dolores Finger, Roslyn Smith, and Linda Brown returned and maintained that, while the four young men at North Carolina A&T sat down first, the action grew from a plan deliberated on Bennett's campus, with Dr. Jarrett urging the girls to invite the A&T men so they would be protected.

chapters.title: Wait Until After Christmas
chapters.summary: Terry recounts that President Willa B. Player would not forbid her girls from acting but cautioned that starting before Christmas vacation would scatter the momentum, so they waited; she recalls Gwen Mackel standing ready in Woolworth's on February 1, having bought something first as trained, when the young men sat down, the culmination of a plan made on campus.

chapters.title: We Were Proud
chapters.summary: Terry confirms she sat at the lunch counter alongside Linda Brown, recalling how proud she felt while her mother was likely terrified, and how unnerved she was by the unexpected hatred glaring at them; she notes her quiet mother said little about it, perhaps regretting the world that made it necessary but never telling her how she felt.

chapters.title: A Big Woman in Stature
chapters.summary: Terry portrays Willa B. Player as a dignified, quietly powerful woman profoundly committed to making Bennett students into strong women grounded in social justice, who believed education was no good unless used to correct wrongs and who lived what she believed without fiery speeches.

chapters.title: Turn In Your Grades
chapters.summary: Terry recalls that Player let her girls march and sit at the counter but required them to keep up their grades with no faculty excuses, a real-stakes stance she contrasts with students elsewhere asking professors to forgive their absences; she says she knew little of the A&T president but believed he must have wanted the Greensboro sit-ins to succeed.

chapters.title: These Are My Girls
chapters.summary: Terry tells how, when the girls were arrested in a later wave and jailed, Willa B. Player drove herself downtown alone, a dangerous act for a Black woman in Greensboro, to declare these are my girls and see after them, and notes that a cross had been burned at the college president's house.

chapters.title: I Wanted to Study English
chapters.summary: Turning to her 1961 graduation, Terry describes how studying with Dr. Jarrett and Dr. Crawford turned her ambition from teaching back home toward becoming an English teacher and going to graduate school, and explains the old practice by which a Black applicant accepted at a Northern university could have the state pay rather than desegregate its own classrooms.

chapters.title: You Can't Be Esther Alexander
chapters.summary: Terry recounts her parents urging her to UNC-Chapel Hill after the sit-ins, a stint as a waitress in Hartford where her sister kept her wages as enforced savings, and her arrival to collect a dorm key, where a woman, stunned that Esther Alexander was Black, realized she had paired her with a white roommate against a UNC ruling and did not know what to do.

chapters.title: It Is the Law, Not the Girls
chapters.summary: Terry describes being given a room alone, prepared to be furious until white girls short of housing offered to room with her, and her mother's lesson that it was the ruling she should hate, not the girls; she earned her master's quietly, taking a class with C. Hugh Holman among gentleman professors, then taught at a college for a couple of years before pursuing the doctorate at UMass Amherst.

chapters.title: Easy in Massachusetts
chapters.summary: Terry turns to the 1969 creation of the Afro-American Studies program at UMass Amherst, which she would later direct, and explains that what happened in Massachusetts could not have happened elsewhere because of a sympathetic administration and an incredible assembly of faculty and friends gathered there.

chapters.title: Sterling's Old Gang
chapters.summary: Terry recounts how the Massachusetts Review, launched in 1959 and dedicated to the four young men who sat down at North Carolina A&T, became her circle, and how she, Michael Thelwell, Bernard Bell, and her future husband Eugene Terry came to Amherst because Sterling Brown of Howard sent his students to study with Sidney Kaplan, who knew Black American literature like no one else and now directed the English graduate program.

chapters.title: Embellishing the American Story
chapters.summary: Terry describes the program they built under the English department and the conviction that animated it, never to erase American literature but to embellish it with the African American contribution as a uniquely American narrative; Michael Thelwell had come from the movement and the work of the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, and the talk turned from a program to a department whose case went to the trustees in Boston.

chapters.title: Nobody Blew Anything Up
chapters.summary: Terry recalls that, unlike the fights at Cornell and elsewhere, the independent Afro-American Studies department was granted quietly with no protest, leaving her almost let down for want of celebratory noise; she reflects that UMass Amherst was a special place where she crossed thresholds others thought she could not, joining Black and white colleagues to build a strong program.

chapters.title: Run the Lap You're In
chapters.summary: Returning as provost in 2009, Terry reflects on Bennett and the young women there, warning that knocking down the old signs can fool people into thinking the work is done; she tells students that one runs only the lap one is in before passing it on, that the world is different but not yet perfect, and that they must find its remaining problems.

chapters.title: Big Women in the Making
chapters.summary: Terry closes by charging students to make social justice and giving back to the community a partner to whatever skills they pursue, reflecting on a younger generation's technology she resists, and saying she hopes Bennett can be a haven that turns hundreds of little girls into big women, work she counts an honor on the days she does it.

loc_video.caption: Esther M. A. Terry oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Greensboro, North Carolina, 2011 July 06
